value.Title: Pettit National Ice Center rooftop solar system installed

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGR-K253-RVRY-T0C1-00000-00Pettit National Ice Center rooftop solar system installed2026-05-01T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisNEWSPettit National Ice Center rooftop solar system installedCary Docter

MILWAUKEE - The Pettit National Ice Center announced on Friday, May 1, that it is embracing a sustainable future with the installation of a 690 kW rooftop solar energy system in partnership with Arch Solar, a Wisconsin-based solar company.

Going solar

What we know:

Featuring 1,180 bi-facial solar panels mounted on custom racking, the array will generate over 815,000 kWh annually, equivalent to running 80,000 resurfacings with a Zamboni.

FREE DOWNLOAD: Get breaking news alerts in the FOX LOCAL Mobile app for iOS or Android

What they're saying:

"More than 400,000 visitors visit the Pettit Center every year. And with that visitation, we have students, we have athletes, we have families. The installation becomes a living classroom and a demonstration of how renewable energy can be used in large ice rink facilities like this," said Paul Golomski, general manager at the Pettit National Ice Center.

"I was driving home last night trying to conceptualize how do you describe 690kW, to the layperson. And I heard a semi truck downshift on the highway, and I was like, I wonder if I could calculate how it would be related to a semi truck running at full speed with a full load. and so 690kW above us is, equivalent to about two semi trucks going at full speed, full torque at about 70 miles an hour down the highway," said Andrew Holmstrom from Arch Solar.

"The solar project will power about 20% of its energy usage. That's a lot of Zamboni runs around the rink. Future Olympians training this facility will be skating on sunshine and striding towards gold for the USA," said Andrew Kell, Policy Director for RENEW Wisconsin.

Dig deeper:

A news release says the new solar array is projected to offset 20% of the facility's annual energy use, reducing operational costs and reinforcing the Pettit Center's role as a responsible community leader. Savings from renewable energy will be reinvested into operations and capital improvements, helping ensure future generations can enjoy this world-class venue that trains United States Olympians.

The Source: Information in this post was provided by the Pettit National Ice Center.

MilwaukeeEnvironmentNews

value.Title: Tribal Energy Alternatives Awards $3.2M in Solar Grants

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JGY-PCP3-RW50-F2CG-00000-00Tribal Energy Alternatives Awards $3.2M in Solar Grants2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:09:17ZLexisNexisTribal Energy Alternatives Awards $3.2M in Solar Grants

Tribal Energy Alternatives has awarded $3.2 million in solar grants to 14 tribal nations and affiliated organizations to support renewable energy deployment and workforce development across Indian Country.

The funding, distributed through the Tribal Solar Accelerator Fund (TSAF) FacilityResidential program, will support solar installations at tribal housing, community facilities and critical infrastructure, along with hands-on training for tribal members.

Recipients include the Confederated Tribes of the Colville Reservation, Hopi Utilities Corporation and the Menominee Indian Tribe of Wisconsin, among others. The program is designed to reduce energy costs, expand local energy capacity and build workforce skills, according to a release.

The latest awards build on prior Tribal Solar Accelerator Fund activity. In November, Tribal Business News reported a $3.6 million funding round supporting 26 tribal recipients across solar deployment, workforce training and energy planning initiatives.

The organization also announced completion of a TSAF-funded solar project with the Cheyenne and Arapaho Tribes in Oklahoma.

The project installed solar systems on 13 homes, totaling 113.4 kilowatts of capacity and expected to generate about 184,591 kilowatt-hours annually. That output is projected to produce roughly $20,400 in annual energy savings for participating households.

The effort also included workforce training for four tribal members through TEA-supported construction and training programs.

The latest awards follow the organizations transition to an independent model. In April 2025, Tribal Energy Alternatives launched as a Native-led affiliate after separating from GRID Alternatives National Tribal Program, with a focus on grant funding, workforce training, project development and policy support for tribal energy initiatives.

The TSAF program, launched in 2019, supports solar deployment in tribal communities through combined infrastructure and workforce investments.

value.Title: Professor emeritus John Ralph elected to National Academy of Sciences

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJP-M043-S035-M2DB-00000-00Professor emeritus John Ralph elected to National Academy of Sciences2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisCOMMUNITYProfessor emeritus John Ralph elected to National Academy of Sciencespdschmitt

The honor recognizes his pioneering work to understand the biochemistry of an important binding compound in plants.

By Renata Solan

May 11, 2026

John Ralph, a biochemistry professor emeritus at the University of Wisconsin–Madison, has been elected to the National Academy of Sciences. Ralph, who retired in 2024, studied lignin structure and biosynthesis pathways in plants.

The National Academy of Sciences is a private, nonprofit institution that was established under a congressional charter signed by President Abraham Lincoln in 1863. It provides science, engineering and health policy advice to the federal government and other organizations. Members of the academy are elected and recognized for their distinguished achievements in original research.

"In addition to the personal honor and the validation of work on the abundant but little-recognized lignin polymer, this recognition acknowledges the accomplishments of an exceptional group of students, postdoctoral researchers and distinguished colleagues from across the globe who have collaborated on significant plant-related projects," says Ralph, who was also an investigator at the Wisconsin Energy Institute and the Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center. "The daily challenges, the exhilaration of novel discoveries and, most importantly, the interaction with a cadre of brilliant young students and exceptional collaborators have rendered this journey profoundly rewarding."

Ralph is internationally recognized for his work on lignin, an aromatic compound that binds together plant sugars, giving structure, strength and rigidity to plant cell walls. Lignin is used in a variety of products, including fuels and medicines, but its variable structure makes it difficult to study and complicated to break down.

Ralph pioneered novel approaches to understanding the biochemistry of lignin structures, including innovative uses of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). Over the course of his career, Ralph has studied a wide range of plants, revealing variability in lignin structure within and among plants and characterizing previously unknown lignin structures.

"It is a great and very well-deserved honor for John to be elected to the National Academy of Sciences for his outstanding work on the structure of plant cell walls. We are proud of his many ground-breaking scientific achievements and we celebrate his election," says Richard Amasino, chair of the Department of Biochemistry, who was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 2006.

In 2024, Ralph and his collaborator, Wout Boerjan, were awarded the prestigious Marcus Wallenberg Prize for their contributions to our understanding of lignin biosynthesis and structural diversity by developing and innovatively applying analytical techniques, opening new opportunities for industrial uses of lignin.

"Lignin, the most abundant source of sustainable aromatics on the planet, remains largely unrecognized and underutilized. Advancements in comprehending its traits have unveiled promising prospects for improvement of crops and trees as well as for lignin's utilization," says Ralph. "I sincerely hope that the field will continue to attract astute young researchers to develop novel knowledge and methodologies that will propel the field forward."

The academy's 2026 election brings the total number of active members to 2,705, including 32 researchers from UW–Madison.

value.Title: PROFESSOR EMERITUS JOHN RALPH ELECTED TO NATIONAL ACADEMY OF SCIENCES

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK1-S8M3-S5H9-J4VP-00000-00PROFESSOR EMERITUS JOHN RALPH ELECTED TO NATIONAL ACADEMY OF SCIENCES2026-05-11T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisPROFESSOR EMERITUS JOHN RALPH ELECTED TO NATIONAL ACADEMY OF SCIENCESStates News ServiceMADISON, Wis.

The following information was released by the University of Wisconsin - Madison:

The honor recognizes his pioneering work to understand the biochemistry of an important binding compound in plants.

​By Renata Solan

John Ralph, a biochemistry professor emeritus at the University of WisconsinMadison, has been elected to the National Academy of Sciences. Ralph, who retired in 2024, studied lignin structure and biosynthesis pathways in plants.

The National Academy of Sciences is a private, nonprofit institution that was established under a congressional charter signed by President Abraham Lincoln in 1863. It provides science, engineering and health policy advice to the federal government and other organizations. Members of the academy are elected and recognized for their distinguished achievements in original research.

"In addition to the personal honor and the validation of work on the abundant but little-recognized lignin polymer, this recognition acknowledges the accomplishments of an exceptional group of students, postdoctoral researchers and distinguished colleagues from across the globe who have collaborated on significant plant-related projects," says Ralph, who was also an investigator at the Wisconsin Energy Institute and the Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center. "The daily challenges, the exhilaration of novel discoveries and, most importantly, the interaction with a cadre of brilliant young students and exceptional collaborators have rendered this journey profoundly rewarding."

Ralph is internationally recognized for his work on lignin, an aromatic compound that binds together plant sugars, giving structure, strength and rigidity to plant cell walls. Lignin is used in a variety of products, including fuels and medicines, but its variable structure makes it difficult to study and complicated to break down.

Ralph pioneered novel approaches to understanding the biochemistry of lignin structures, including innovative uses of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). Over the course of his career, Ralph has studied a wide range of plants, revealing variability in lignin structure within and among plants and characterizing previously unknown lignin structures.

"It is a great and very well-deserved honor for John to be elected to the National Academy of Sciences for his outstanding work on the structure of plant cell walls. We are proud of his many ground-breaking scientific achievements and we celebrate his election," says Richard Amasino, chair of the Department of Biochemistry, who was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 2006.

In 2024, Ralph and his collaborator, Wout Boerjan, were awarded the prestigious Marcus Wallenberg Prize for their contributions to our understanding of lignin biosynthesis and structural diversity by developing and innovatively applying analytical techniques, opening new opportunities for industrial uses of lignin.

"Lignin, the most abundant source of sustainable aromatics on the planet, remains largely unrecognized and underutilized. Advancements in comprehending its [traits] have unveiled promising prospects for improvement of crops and trees as well as for lignin's utilization," says Ralph. "I sincerely hope that the field will continue to attract astute young researchers to develop novel knowledge and methodologies that will propel the field forward."

The academy's 2026 election brings the total number of active members to 2,705, including 32 researchers from UWMadison.

The University of Wisconsin posted the following news:

* * *

Professor Emeritus John Ralph elected to National Academy of Sciences

*

The honor recognizes his pioneering work to understand the biochemistry of an important binding compound in plants.

By Renata Solan

John Ralph, a biochemistry professor emeritus at the University of Wisconsin-Madison, has been elected to the National Academy of Sciences. Ralph, who retired in 2024, studied lignin structure and biosynthesis pathways in plants.

The National Academy of Sciences is a private, nonprofit institution that was established under a congressional charter signed by President Abraham Lincoln in 1863. It provides science, engineering and health policy advice to the federal government and other organizations. Members of the academy are elected and recognized for their distinguished achievements in original research.

"In addition to the personal honor and the validation of work on the abundant but little-recognized lignin polymer, this recognition acknowledges the accomplishments of an exceptional group of students, postdoctoral researchers and distinguished colleagues from across the globe who have collaborated on significant plant-related projects," says Ralph, who was also an investigator at the Wisconsin Energy Institute and the Great Lakes Bioenergy Research Center. "The daily challenges, the exhilaration of novel discoveries and, most importantly, the interaction with a cadre of brilliant young students and exceptional collaborators have rendered this journey profoundly rewarding."

Ralph is internationally recognized for his work on lignin, an aromatic compound that binds together plant sugars, giving structure, strength and rigidity to plant cell walls. Lignin is used in a variety of products, including fuels and medicines, but its variable structure makes it difficult to study and complicated to break down.

Ralph pioneered novel approaches to understanding the biochemistry of lignin structures, including innovative uses of nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR). Over the course of his career, Ralph has studied a wide range of plants, revealing variability in lignin structure within and among plants and characterizing previously unknown lignin structures.

"It is a great and very well-deserved honor for John to be elected to the National Academy of Sciences for his outstanding work on the structure of plant cell walls. We are proud of his many ground-breaking scientific achievements and we celebrate his election," says Richard Amasino, chair of the Department of Biochemistry, who was elected to the National Academy of Sciences in 2006.

In 2024, Ralph and his collaborator, Wout Boerjan, were awarded the prestigious Marcus Wallenberg Prize for their contributions to our understanding of lignin biosynthesis and structural diversity by developing and innovatively applying analytical techniques, opening new opportunities for industrial uses of lignin.

"Lignin, the most abundant source of sustainable aromatics on the planet, remains largely unrecognized and underutilized. Advancements in comprehending its [traits] have unveiled promising prospects for improvement of crops and trees as well as for lignin's utilization," says Ralph. "I sincerely hope that the field will continue to attract astute young researchers to develop novel knowledge and methodologies that will propel the field forward."

The academy's 2026 election brings the total number of active members to 2,705, including 32 researchers from UW-Madison.

value.Title: Medline announces completion of new commercial solar installation in Grayslake , Ill .

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJN-TBT3-S5W1-H0C7-00000-00Medline announces completion of new commercial solar installation in Grayslake, Ill.2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisMedline announces completion of new commercial solar installation in Grayslake, Ill.

Key Highlights:

* 11,500 solar panels produce 5,900 megawatt hours of energy per year.

* 12th solar project for Medline, adding to 59,000 existing solar panels.

* Medline invests in distribution center and manufacturing facility resiliency.

Original Press Release:

Grayslake, Illinois, May 5 -- Medline issued the following news release:

- Ceremony marks Medline's 12th solar installation, which adds more than 11,500 panels

Medline (Nasdaq: MDLN), the largest provider of medical-surgical products and supply chain solutions serving all points of care, and PowerFlex, a clean-technology solutions company focused on making transformations to carbon-free electrification and transportation possible, recently unveiled a completed 5.2-megawatt solar installation at Medline's flagship distribution center in Grayslake, Ill.

The investment includes more than 11,500 solar panels and is projected to produce 5,900 megawatt hours of energy per year. This installation is Medline's 12th solar project and adds to the more than 59,000 existing solar panels at Medline facilities as of Dec. 31, 2025.

"Medline is proud to support a more resilient healthcare system," said Francesa Olivier, vice president of sustainability. "This latest installation advances our efforts to reduce environmental impact across our operations while making healthcare run better."

Added Elizabeth Davies, mayor of the Village of Grayslake, "Since establishing its distribution center in Grayslake, Medline has demonstrated its commitment to being an integral part of our community. Innovations such as the new solar panel array highlight Medline's dedication to responsible growth and respect for the unique character of Grayslake. These investments empower our community to flourish while preserving and celebrating its rich heritage."

PowerFlex was also on hand at the unveiling to speak about the years-long relationship between the two companies.

"Seeing this project come to life at Medline's Grayslake facility is an exciting milestone for both of our teams," said Nate McMurry, chief commercial officer at PowerFlex. "What began as a shared vision is now a large-scale rooftop solar installation that will help power one of Medline's key distribution centers with renewable energy. This project reflects how strong partnerships can turn ambitious sustainability goals into real operational impact, and we're proud to support Medline as it continues investing in a more resilient and energy-efficient future."

A vital piece of Medline's distribution network of 45 facilities across the U.S., the 1.4 million-square-foot Grayslake site opened its doors in 2022 and has grown to provide more than 900 jobs in the community. Deliveries from Grayslake are made to healthcare customers throughout Illinois, Michigan, Wisconsin and the northern half of Indiana.

"Medline continues to invest in the resiliency of the supply chain, and this is one of the latest examples as part of our investment in our distribution centers and manufacturing facilities, to not only make healthcare run better, but to reflect thoughtful energy choices across our operations," said Casey Guth, vice president of regional field sales for Medline.

At the conclusion of the unveiling event, attendees and Medline employees were invited to sign a ceremonial solar panel that will be installed in the lobby of the Grayslake facility as a visual reminder of the project.

About Medline

Medline is the largest provider of medical-surgical products and supply chain solutions serving all points of care. Through its broad product portfolio, resilient supply chain and leading clinical solutions, Medline helps healthcare providers improve their clinical, financial and operational outcomes. Headquartered in Northfield, Ill., the company employs more than 45,000 people worldwide and operates in more than 100 countries. To learn more about how Medline makes healthcare run better, visit www.medline.com.

Unless otherwise indicated, all figures are as of Dec. 31, 2025.

[Category: Renewable Energy, Healthcare, Sustainability Initiatives, ESG and Sustainability, CSR]

Source: Medline

value.Title: Solar project ribbon cutting - The Clarion
value.Overview: The Clarion: Madison Area Technical College University Wire, NEWS; Pg. 1, 382words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH7-9H13-RXVN-63NG-00000-00Solar project ribbon cutting - The Clarion2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisNEWSPg. 1Solar project ribbon cutting - The Clarion

Madison College recently marked a sustainability milestone with a ribbon-cutting ceremony for its new solar plus energy storage system at the Truax Campus Protective Services Building.

The solar project is designed to enhance reliability, reduce environmental impact and help lower operating costs.

Along with these benefits, the system also offers a hands-on learning laboratory for students and educators.

"At our campuses, students gain hands-on experience with innovative technologies that prepare them for clean energy careers," said Dr. Jennifer Berne, Madison College president. "At the same time, we are advancing sustainable, reliable energy solutions for our region."

The April 20 ribbon-cutting ceremony was part of a series of "Earth Week" events held at the college. Other activities included a sustainability tour, free bike tune-ups, a sustainability fair and trash pick-up throughout the campus.

Features of the new large-scale battery energy storage installation at the Protective Services Building include:

180 kW DC ground-mounted solar array with 360 bifacial panels.

Double-sided solar panels to boost energy production by 10 to 20 percent.

Battery energy storage that operates in temperatures as low as 1 degree Fahrenheit.

Integration with geothermal heating and cooling for a fully electric, high-efficiency building system.

A more than 30-year lifespan with an estimated eight- to 10-year payback.

The project will offer an opportunity for students to study solar performance in programs such as Renewable Energy Certificate, Electrical Apprenticeship, Electrical Technical Diploma, Construction Technical Diploma, Industrial Maintenance AS, Electromechanical Technology AS and Architecture AS.

"This solar plus storage installation provides infrastructure for training students for skilled technical careers in the energy workforce," said Madison College instructor Ken Walz.

The $665,000 solar plus energy storage project was supported in part by a $435,000 award from the Wisconsin Energy Innovation Grant Program.

"The project demonstrates Madison College's commitment to protecting our planet, responsibly stewarding our operations and advancing our mission to serve students," said Dr. Sylvia Ramirez, the college's executive vice president of finance and administration.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHK-BKC3-S0RF-C55B-00000-00Federal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 102026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisFederal Energy Regulatory Commission: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation submits tariff filing per 35.13(a)(2)(iii: 2026-05-05 Plum Creek E&P - 793 - 0.0.0 to be effective 4/28/2026 under ER26-2461 Filing Type : 10

Washington: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation has submitted the following document to the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission:

The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseSecretaryFederal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426RE: Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporationRevised Engineering & Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104Service Agreement No. 793-NSPDocket No. ER26-____-000Dear Secretary Reese:Pursuant to Section 205 of the Federal Power Act, 16 U.S.C § 824d (2018), and Section 35.13 of the regulations of the Federal Energy Regulatory Commission (“Commission”), 18 C.F.R § 35. 13 (2026), Northern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation (“NSPM”), hereby submits for filing the Revised Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (“Revised Agreement”) between NSPM, Geronimo Power, LLC (“Geronimo”), and Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”). Individually, NSPM, Geronimo, and MISO are referred to herein as “Party” and collectively as the “Parties”. The Agreement is submitted in accordance with Order No. 7141 and the Commission ’ s eTariff filing requirements.1. BackgroundNSPM and its utility operating company affiliate Northern States Power Company, a Wisconsin corporation (together the “NSP Companies”) are wholly owned utility operating company subsidiaries of Xcel Energy Inc., and are engaged in the business of generating, transmitting, distributing and selling electric power and energy and related services in the States of Minnesota, Michigan, North Dakota, South Dakota, and Wisconsin. The NSP Companies are transmission-owning members of the Midcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc. (“MISO”) and provide most transmission service pursuant to the MISO Open Access Transmission, Energy and Operating Reserve Markets Tariff on file with and accepted by the Commission. However, certain services are, or have been, provided pursuant to individual grandfathered agreements or the Xcel Energy Operating Companies Open Access Transmission Tariff (“Xcel Energy OATT”) on file with and accepted by the Commission.2 Individual NSPM service agreements are on file with the Commission in NSPM ’ s FERC Electric Tariff Second Revised Volume No. 3.1 Electronic Tariff Filings, Order No. 714, 124 FERC ¶ 61,270 (2008); order on clarification, Order No. 714-A, 147 FERC ¶ 61,115 (2014).2 The Xcel Energy OATT was restated in eTariff and accepted for filing in Public Service Company of Colorado, Docket No. ER16-1422-000, unpublished letter order (August 16, 2016).The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 2 of 6Geronimo is a wholly owned subsidiary of National Grid Renewables, a leading renewable energy company with a nationwide development portfolio. Under National Grid Renewables ’ ownership, Geronimo has continued its core focus on project origination and early-stage development while leveraging broader construction, financing, and asset management capabilities.On April 28, 2026, the Parties executed the Revised Agreement modifying certain provisions of the Engineering and Procurement Agreement for MISO Project J2104 (Original Agreement). The Original Agreement was executed as a conforming agreement under Attachment W-1 of the Xcel Energy OATT, effective June 20, 2025.2. The Instant FilingThe modifications to the Original Agreement proposed herein reflect necessary changes that occurred following its execution, including an updated in-service date, an extension of the termination provision in absence of a Generator Interconnection Agreement, and an increase to the security amount associated with upgrades required to accommodate MISO Project J2104. In addition, the Revised Agreement includes minor administrative modifications that may be considered non-conforming. Accordingly, the Revised Agreement is being filed out of an abundance of caution.Section 14 and Appendix A identify the minor modifications that NSPM deems to be non-conforming. Consistent with filing requirements, NSPM submits the Revised Agreement in both redline and clean formats.3. Proposed Effective Date and Request for Waiver of NoticeNSPM respectfully requests that the Commission grant waiver of its sixty (60) day prior notice requirement3 and allow the Revised Agreement to become effective on April 28, 2026, the date of execution. NSPM submits that good cause exists to grant the requested waiver. It is the Commission ’ s policy to allow waiver of the prior notice requirements for service agreements under a tariff filed within thirty (30) days of the requested effective date.4 Here, the Revised Agreement is being filed within 7 days of its requested effective date of April 28, 2026. Moreover, the Parties support an April 28, 2026, effective date for the Revised Agreement. NSPM requests April 28, 2026, to provide certainty to the Parties as to the status of the Revised Agreement.4. Description of Documents Being Submitted with Filing5a. This transmittal letter,b. A clean version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP, andc. A redline version of Service Agreement No. 793-NSP.3 18 C.F.R § 35.3(a) (2026).4 Prior Notice and Filing Requirements under Part II of the Federal Power Act, 64 FERC ¶ 61,139 at 61,984 (1993). See also PJM Interconnection, LLC, 194 FERC ¶ 61,096 at P 14 (2026).5 The Original Agreement was reported on the EQR as 778-NSP. This eTariff filing designates the Revised Agreement as 793-NSP.The Honorable Debbie-Anne A. ReeseMay 5, 2026Page 3 of 65. Communications and CorrespondenceCommunications and correspondence with respect to this filing should be directed to each of the following: 6Service and PostingAn electronic version or notice of this filing will be served upon each of the state commissions having jurisdiction over NSPM and on representatives of the Parties. A courtesy copy will be provided to the Division of Electric Power Regulation (Central). In addition, a copy of this filing will be posted at the offices of NSPM at 414 Nicollet Mall in Minneapolis, Minnesota.Please direct any questions regarding this filing to Elizabeth Walkup at (612) 216-8223.Respectfully submitted,/s/ Jonathan EserJonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.On behalf ofNorthern States Power Company, a Minnesota corporation6 NSPM requests waiver of Section 385.203(b)(3) of the Commission ’ s regulations to permit the designation of more than two persons upon whom service is to be made in this proceeding. 18 C.F.R § 385.203(b)(3) (2026).Jonathan EserAssistant General Counsel & Senior Director, Transmission Business RelationsXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Email: Jonathan.Eser@xcelenergy.comMichèle B. BrüningPrincipal Transmission RepresentativeXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401Phone: (612) 330-5836Email: Michele.B.Bruning@xcelenergy.comJoseph W. LowellAssistant General CounselXcel Energy Services Inc.701 Pennsylvania Ave. NW Suite 250Washington, DC 20004Phone: (202) 661-4491Email: Joe.W.Lowell@xcelenergy.comSERVICE LISTMs. Natalie Tingle-StewartDirectorDivision of Electric Power Regulation (Central)Federal Energy Regulatory Commission888 First Street, NEWashington, DC 20426Email: natalie.tingle-stewart@ferc.govMr Mike BullActing Executive SecretaryMinnesota Public Utilities Commission121 7th Place E., Suite 350Saint Paul, MN 55101-2147Email: mike.bull@state.mn.usMs Patricia Van GerpenExecutive DirectorSouth Dakota Public Utilities Commission500 E. Capitol Ave.Pierre, SD 57501-5070Email: patty.vangerpen@state.sd.usMr Steve KahlExecutive SecretaryNorth Dakota Public Service Commission600 E Boulevard, Dept. 408Bismarck, ND 58505-0480Email: pscexecutivesec@nd.govChris AyikaInterconnection ManagerGeronimo Power8400 Normandale Lake Boulevard, Suite 1200Bloomington, MN 55437Email: cayika@geronimopower.com952-641-4031Kalen ColemanMidcontinent Independent System Operator, Inc.720 City Center Dr.Carmel, IN 46032Telephone: (317) 219-8481kcoleman@misoenergy.orgagreements@misoenergy.orgCERTIFICATE OF SERVICEI, Elizabeth Walkup, hereby certify that I have on this day served a notice of the enclosed filing via electronic mail on each party designated on the attached Service List.Dated at Minneapolis, Minnesota this 5th day of May 2026./s/ Elizabeth WalkupTransmission Account Rep and Technical SpecialistXcel Energy Services Inc.414 Nicollet Mall, 414-6Minneapolis, MN 55401 Phone: (612) 216-8223Email: elizabeth.a.walkup@xcelenergy.comA

value.Title: US: Microsoft wants to ditch energy-saving commitments to allow AI to thrive

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-DBG3-SBTP-009S-00000-00US: Microsoft wants to ditch energy-saving commitments to allow AI to thrive2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:12:36ZLexisNexisUS: Microsoft wants to ditch energy-saving commitments to allow AI to thrive

According to sources familiar with the matter, Microsoft may abandon one of the industry's most ambitious clean energy goals. The company previously planned to source 100% of its hourly electricity from renewable energy by 2030. The corporation's shift in environmental direction should improve its competitiveness in the race to power data centers.

Costly and energy-intensive data center construction is changing companies' views on the feasibility of climate commitments made before the AI era. A Microsoft spokesperson stated that the company continues to explore ways to maintain its annual co-financing target, without commenting on the much more stringent hourly consumption commitment. Discussions within Microsoft are ongoing, and no final decision has been made yet.

Major tech companies have previously publicly announced unusually ambitious emissions reduction targets, with Microsoft even pledging to remove more carbon dioxide from the atmosphere than it emits. But as companies like Amazon and Meta struggle to secure sufficient energy to meet the demands of artificial intelligence, the appeal of fossil fuels is rapidly growing. In the race to launch data centers as quickly as possible, clean energy goals are becoming secondary.

Microsoft's flagship clean energy goal, announced in 2021—dubbed 100/100/0—called for the company to offset 100% of its electricity consumption, 100% of the time, through the purchase of zero-carbon energy. This commitment to purchase electricity equivalent to a year's consumption was significantly more ambitious than Microsoft's previous goal, which the company had already achieved—purchasing enough renewable energy to cover its annual electricity consumption.

In their latest sustainability reports, Meta, Google, Amazon, and Microsoft said their carbon emissions had increased by 64%, 51%, 33%, and 23%, respectively, compared to levels before ChatGPT's initial launch in late 2022. Microsoft cited "growth factors such as AI and the expansion of cloud computing" as reasons for the sharp rise in emissions.

According to Bloomberg, over the next decade, the amount of electricity consumed by US data centers is likely to more than double to 106 gigawatts, with natural gas playing a key role. Globally, renewable energy sources will only cover 50% of the growth in data center electricity demand.

Microsoft adds about a gigawatt of data center capacity every three months—enough to power 750,000 homes. As of February, Microsoft had signed contracts to supply over 40 gigawatts of renewable energy. The company recently signed agreements to connect an additional 1.2 gigawatts of zero-carbon energy projects to the grid in Wisconsin, beginning in late 2028. Microsoft is developing one of its largest AI data centers in the state. The company was also in talks to finance a large natural gas processing plant in the Permian Basin of West Texas.

Microsoft is facing serious financial constraints. The company plans to spend $190 billion by the end of December, primarily on data centers. Rising AI costs have led to budget cuts in many departments, including those focused on carbon reduction, prompting a more careful review of financial commitments to clean energy projects.

"Artificial intelligence is a battle for survival for big tech companies, so every bit of money they have is being directed toward creating as much AI as possible," said a representative of the High Tide Foundation.

According to people familiar with the program, achieving the 100/100/0 goal within Microsoft was always considered a difficult task. Google, which called its own similar plan a "breakthrough" goal, stated in its latest sustainability report that it had achieved carbon-free energy use in approximately two-thirds of its operations.

Hourly energy matching involves companies purchasing sufficient clean energy or renewable energy certificates to offset the total number of megawatt-hours of electricity they consume over a given period. This approach is a popular strategy for companies seeking to meet their climate change commitments. Critics of hourly matching argue that such programs can actually discourage investment in renewable energy by creating additional budget burdens and administrative costs that many companies cannot afford. (3dnews.ru)

value.Title: Proposal: Limits on AI in the workplace Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace
value.Overview: Wisconsin State Journal, A; Pg. A3, 735words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH5-BR33-RV78-61NT-00000-00Proposal: Limits on AI in the workplace2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisAPg. A3Proposal: Limits on AI in the workplaceWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

GOVERNOR'S RACE | FRANCESCA HONG

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

Hong's AI proposal would limit "robo bosses" by requiring employers to disclose to employees or prospective employees when automated tools are used to make human resource decisions. It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is - and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges - assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers - enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs - were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

Top stories for May 4, 2026, in the Wisconsin State Journal Hong USA Today Network

value.Title: Microsoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand Surge

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHT-FN43-S35T-Y3YW-00000-00Microsoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand Surge2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:10:15ZLexisNexisMicrosoft May Revise 2030 Renewable Energy Target Amid AI Demand SurgeBW Online Bureau

Rapid expansion of AI infrastructure and data centres is increasing pressure on Microsoft's long-term renewable energy commitments

Microsoft is considering revising its 2030 renewable energy target as rising electricity demand from artificial intelligence and data centres puts pressure on its climate commitments, according to a Bloomberg report.

The report said the rapid growth of energy-intensive artificial intelligence infrastructure and data centres is reshaping the feasibility of climate commitments that were made before the current AI expansion. Discussions within the company are said to be ongoing, and no final decision has been taken.

A Microsoft spokesperson said the company continued to explore opportunities to maintain its renewable energy matching goal. The spokesperson pointed to recently signed agreements with We Eneries to support 1.2 gigawatts of carbon-free energy projects in Wisconsin, including solar and battery storage facilities expected to begin operations from late 2028.

Like several large technology companies, Microsoft has significantly increased investments in artificial intelligence infrastructure in recent years. The company is spending heavily on expanding the data centre capacity required to support products such as Copilot and its Azure cloud platform.

The growing energy needs of artificial intelligence systems are creating new challenges for technology companies attempting to meet climate and renewable energy commitments. Some of the data centres currently under development by major technology firms are expected to require multiple gigawatts of electricity capacity. Industry estimates suggest that one gigawatt of power is sufficient to supply electricity to roughly 750,000 homes in the United States.

The surge in electricity demand has triggered increased investment across energy sectors, including renewable energy, battery storage, natural gas and nuclear power. Industry executives have argued that gas-fired power projects can often be deployed faster than renewable energy infrastructure, particularly as demand from data centres rises sharply.

In 2024, Microsoft signed a power agreement with Constellation Energy linked to the planned revival of a unit at the Three Mile Island nuclear plant in Pennsylvania. The developments reflect the growing tension between the rapid expansion of artificial intelligence infrastructure and the climate targets adopted by global technology companies over the past decade.

urn:contentItem:6JH6-MY43-RTGG-S2HW-00000-00Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisSTATE REGIONALWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

Hong's AI proposal would limit "robo bosses" by requiring employers to disclose to employees or prospective employees when automated tools are used to make human resource decisions. It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans.

Special electric rates set for data centers in a first for Wisconsin

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

Surplus deal, other bills in limbo as Wisconsin legislative session winds down

Wisconsin utilities join effort to curb power line competition

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans.

Special electric rates set for data centers in a first for Wisconsin

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

urn:contentItem:6JH6-0NJ3-SHTX-R47B-00000-00Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace2026-05-04T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisLAKEGENEVANEWS.NETWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

"Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

value.Overview: AWP OTS (Original text service) - English, CORPORATE PERFORMANCE; ADHOC-NEWS; Energy; Oil and Gas, 608words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-XM23-RS62-Y3TS-00000-00BUSINESSWIRE : MGE Energy Reports First-Quarter 2026 Earnings2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisCORPORATE PERFORMANCE; ADHOC-NEWSEnergy; Oil and GasBUSINESSWIRE : MGE Energy Reports First-Quarter 2026 Earnings

MGE Energy, Inc. (Nasdaq: MGEE), today reported financial results for the first quarter of 2026.

MGE Energy's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) earnings for the first quarter of 2026 were $48.5 million, or $1.32 per share, compared to $41.6 million, or $1.14 per share, for the same period in the prior year.

Electric segment earnings increased $5.5 million for 2026 compared to 2025, a result of strategic capital investments that grew rate base. This growth remains largely driven by the successful deployment of key renewable energy projects.

Gas net income exhibited steady performance, with minimal variation compared to the first quarter of 2025.

About MGE Energy

MGE Energy is a public utility holding company. Its principal subsidiary, Madison Gas and Electric, generates and distributes electricity to 170,000 customers in Dane County, Wis., and purchases and distributes natural gas to 180,000 customers in seven south-central and western Wisconsin counties. MGE's roots in the Madison area date back more than 150 years.

value.Title: Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH9-GYY3-S9NN-T072-00000-00Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisLAKEGENEVANEWS.NETWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

Hong's AI proposal would limit "robo bosses" by requiring employers to disclose to employees or prospective employees when automated tools are used to make human resource decisions. It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans. A spokesperson for Hong said the policies could be backed by the state's employment, consumer protection and deceptive trade practices laws.

Wisconsin Manufacturers and Commerce, the state's largest business lobbyist, declined to comment, stating they were still reviewing the proposals.

Special electric rates set for data centers in a first for Wisconsin

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

Surplus deal, other bills in limbo as Wisconsin legislative session winds down

Wisconsin utilities join effort to curb power line competition

value.Title: Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace
value.Overview: The Journal Times (Racine, Wisconsin), STATE REGIONAL, 812words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-GGR3-RSCB-90G7-00000-00Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisSTATE REGIONALWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

Hong's AI proposal would limit "robo bosses" by requiring employers to disclose to employees or prospective employees when automated tools are used to make human resource decisions. It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans. A spokesperson for Hong said the policies could be backed by the state's employment, consumer protection and deceptive trade practices laws.

Wisconsin Manufacturers and Commerce, the state's largest business lobbyist, declined to comment, stating they were still reviewing the proposals.

Special electric rates set for data centers in a first for Wisconsin

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

Surplus deal, other bills in limbo as Wisconsin legislative session winds down

Wisconsin utilities join effort to curb power line competition

value.Title: May 04, 2026: SC Johnson Expands OFF! and Raid Outdoor Line as It Pursues Space Bag and Babyganics Deals

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJX-3853-RVJC-C19W-00000-00May 04, 2026: SC Johnson Expands OFF! and Raid Outdoor Line as It Pursues Space Bag and Babyganics Deals2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: SC Johnson Expands OFF! and Raid Outdoor Line as It Pursues Space Bag and Babyganics Deals

SC Johnson & Son is rolling out new outdoor products under its OFF! and Raid brands to help customers enjoy patios, lawns, and gardens this season. This announcement was made on 7th May 2026. The OFF! assortment includes formulations aimed at controlling mosquitoes, ants, and wasps, with prices starting at $5 as spring and summer activities approach.

In addition to the new OFF! and Raid offerings, SC Johnson disclosed plans to acquire the Space Bag brand from ITW, with finalization expected in coming months pending U.S. regulatory approvals; terms were not disclosed. The company has also acquired Babyganics, expanding its presence in family and personal care with products spanning skin care, bath care, insect repellent and cleaning.

INDEX

SECTION 1 SC JOHNSON & SON PROFILE

SECTION 2 SC JOHNSON & SON TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 3 SC JOHNSON & SON RANKING

SECTION 1 SC JOHNSON & SON PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

SC Johnson & Son, founded in 1886 in Racine, Wisconsin, is a family-owned consumer goods company. With $11 billion in revenue and 13,000 employees, it manufactures household brands like Windex, Glade, and Ziploc, operating in 70 countries. SC Johnson emphasizes sustainability, using renewable energy and reducing plastic waste. Its innovation center develops eco-friendly products, and its transparency in ingredient disclosure sets industry standards. Led by H. Fisk Johnson, the companys philanthropy includes land conservation and education grants. SC Johnsons commitment to quality and environmental responsibility reinforces its global leadership in household goods.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JK4-J7R3-RS50-S405-00000-00Rexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) Acquires Revere Electric Supply to Expand Midwest Presence2026-05-14T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisRexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) Acquires Revere Electric Supply to Expand Midwest Presence

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

13 May 2026 15:01 EDT

Rexel USA (OTC: RXEEY (ADR)) completes the acquisition of Revere Electric Supply, a prominent electrical distributor based in Mokena, Illinois. The deal closed on May 13, 2026, and expands Rexel USA's regional footprint by aligning with a service-oriented supplier known for strong customer relationships and technical expertise.

Founded in 1919, Revere Electric Supply operates 10 locations across NorthernIllinois, Central Illinois, Southeast Wisconsin, and Northwest Indiana, and employs 375 people. The company specializes in industrial automation and maintains long-standing relationships with electrical contractors, underpinning its standing in the Midwest industrial market.

Under the arrangement, Revere will retain its name and local identity, ensuring continuity for customers and partners. Rexel USA will preserve Revere's culture and leadership team, with John Cady continuing as President and taking on expanded responsibilities for Rexel's broader Midwest operations, supported by Rexel Midwest Region President Hani Masoudi. Rexel USA Chief Executive Roger Little framed the acquisition as a strategic move to strengthen the company's position in a critical geographic area, while acknowledging Revere's capabilities and customer focus.

IN TODAY'S REPORT:

SECTION 1 REXEL ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Rexel Activities

Activities

Rexel is a leading global distributor of electrical, automation, and digital solutions, headquartered in France, with operations spanning over 30 countries. The company's core business activities center on supplying a wide range of products, including electrical wires, cables, lighting, HVAC systems, and automation technologies, primarily to professionals in the construction, industrial, and services sectors. It also provides value-added services such as technical consulting, project management, and digital tools to enhance efficiency for its customers. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy solutions and digital platforms, fueled by rising demand for sustainable technologies and smart building systems. This growth is supported by Rexel's strategic investments in e-commerce and energy management, positioning the company to capitalize on global trends toward electrification and decarbonization. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 10th largest Electronics company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace
value.Overview: Kenosha News: Web Edition Articles (Wisconsin), STATE REGIONAL, 812words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-19T3-RVM1-00BP-00000-00Wisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplace2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisSTATE REGIONALWisconsin candidate for governor Francesca Hong proposes limitations on AI in the workplaceKIMBERLY WETHAL kwethal@madison.com

Democratic gubernatorial candidate Francesca Hong is proposing limits on artificial intelligence in the workplace as part of her emerging technology platform, including prohibiting it from being used to surveil workers while off duty and eliminating tax breaks for companies that use AI to justify layoffs.

Hong, a self-avowed democratic socialist and one of seven Democrats running in a wide-open race to be the state's next top executive, announced the proposal Friday as a complement to her data center platform, which calls for a moratorium on building data centers until environmental and energy impacts are better understood.

Hong's AI proposal would limit "robo bosses" by requiring employers to disclose to employees or prospective employees when automated tools are used to make human resource decisions. It would also ban the technology from monitoring workers outside of work or in non-work areas and make it illegal to use AI to set dynamic wages or make last-minute scheduling decisions.

The proposal would also prohibit layoffs "on the public dime" by barring any companies that get tax credits or contract with the state from using AI technology as a reason to lay off staff.

"Silicon Valley marketers and data center landlords hype AI as the most significant economic transformation since the Industrial Revolution," Hong said in her policy announcement. "Whether that's true or not, CEOs and corporations are acting like it is and we're seeing the consequences in how they treat their workers."

Like many proposed business regulations, such policies would likely face legal challenges assuming they could even pass the Legislature, currently controlled by Republicans. A spokesperson for Hong said the policies could be backed by the state's employment, consumer protection and deceptive trade practices laws.

Wisconsin Manufacturers and Commerce, the state's largest business lobbyist, declined to comment, stating they were still reviewing the proposals.

Special electric rates set for data centers in a first for Wisconsin

Data centers

In a previous gubernatorial candidate forum in October, candidates offered some support for data center construction but all stressed that long-term planning and the state's energy grid's capability to handle such large developments should be considered.

In addition to a temporary ban on new data centers, Hong has called for using renewable energy to power the vast facilities and would prohibit the use of local or state tax breaks in their construction.

U.S. Rep. Tom Tiffany, the Republican frontrunner, said he is against data centers "destroying farmland" and increasing costs for utility ratepayers.

Responses from multiple Marquette Law School state polls in the last year show bipartisan distrust of data centers and artificial intelligence. In an October 2025 poll, about 55% of Wisconsinites polled across the political spectrum said any benefits from data center construction didn't outweigh the risks.

Just four months later, in February, following a handful of high-profile and secretive data center proposals, including one near DeForest that was ultimately defeated, that average disapproval rating for data centers rose to 70%. Republican skepticism was steady but 76% of independents and 85% of Democrats said the costs were greater, citing strains on water and energy resources.

Demand grows

Data centers enormous facilities that require considerable power to store, process and distribute applications, oftentimes artificial intelligence programs were once a rarity in Wisconsin. But in recent years, as usage of the technology by the general public, especially generative AI, has increased, the demand for data servers across the country, and access to power and water to operate and cool them, has intensified.

Wisconsin has at least 50 data centers, according to the industry-tracking website Data Center Map, with several being proposed or in the process of being constructed. The size and cost of those facilities can vary considerably.

State lawmakers attempted to regulate data centers this session but the efforts failed. A Republican-authored bill that would have required data center developers to cover their energy costs sped through the Assembly but stalled in the Senate after it was pulled from the calendar.

Hong is in a packed field of Democratic candidates running for a spot on the November ballot. Other liberals in the race include Lt. Gov. Sara Rodriguez; Milwaukee County Executive David Crowley; former DOA Secretary Joel Brennan; former Wisconsin Economic Development Corp. secretary and CEO Missy Hughes; former Lt. Gov. Mandela Barnes; and state Sen. Kelda Roys, of Madison. Gov. Tony Evers declined to run for a third term.

Surplus deal, other bills in limbo as Wisconsin legislative session winds down

Wisconsin utilities join effort to curb power line competition
