May 4th, 2026 ( Transcript Daily - Delivered by Newstex )

Environmental Power ( OTCMKTS:EPGRQ - Get Free Report) and Montauk Renewables ( NASDAQ:MNTK - Get Free Report) are both energy companies, but which is the superior business? We will contrast the two businesses based on the strength of their profitability, analyst recommendations, institutional ownership, dividends, risk, earnings and valuation.

Profitability

This table compares Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables' net margins, return on equity and return on assets.

Net MarginsReturn on EquityReturn on AssetsEnvironmental PowerN/AN/AN/AMontauk Renewables0.15%0.10%0.07%

Institutional & Insider Ownership

16.4% of Montauk Renewables shares are owned by institutional investors. 18.8% of Environmental Power shares are owned by company insiders. Comparatively, 54.3% of Montauk Renewables shares are owned by company insiders. Strong institutional ownership is an indication that endowments, large money managers and hedge funds believe a company is poised for long-term growth.

Earnings & Valuation

This table compares Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables"s revenue, earnings per share and valuation.

Gross RevenuePrice/Sales RatioNet IncomeEarnings Per SharePrice/Earnings RatioEnvironmental PowerN/AN/AN/AN/AN/AMontauk Renewables$176.38 million1.11$1.75 million$0.0268.50

Montauk Renewables has higher revenue and earnings than Environmental Power.

Analyst Ratings

This is a summary of recent recommendations and price targets for Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables, as provided by MarketBeat.com.

Sell RatingsHold RatingsBuy RatingsStrong Buy RatingsRating ScoreEnvironmental Power00000.00Montauk Renewables13012.20

Montauk Renewables has a consensus target price of $1.80, suggesting a potential upside of 31.39%. Given Montauk Renewables' stronger consensus rating and higher possible upside, analysts clearly believe Montauk Renewables is more favorable than Environmental Power.

Summary

Montauk Renewables beats Environmental Power on 9 of the 9 factors compared between the two stocks.

About Environmental Power

( Get Free Report)

Environmental Power Corporation engages in the development, ownership, and operation of renewable energy production facilities in the United States. The company develops renewable energy facilities for the production and commercial application of methane-rich biogas produced from animal, food industry, and other organic wastes. The biogas could be sold to end-users or used to produce pipeline-grade methane, which is referred as renewable natural gas, liquefied natural gas, compressed natural gas, and renewable electrical energy or thermal energy, as well as to produce other useful by-products. Environmental Power Corporation operates three single digester facilities in Wisconsin. The company was founded in 1982 and is headquartered in Tarrytown, New York.

About Montauk Renewables

( Get Free Report)

Montauk Renewables, Inc., a renewable energy company, engages in recovery and processing of biogas from landfills and other non-fossil fuel sources. It operates in two segments, Renewable Natural Gas and Renewable Electricity Generation. The company develops, owns, and operates renewable natural gas (RNG) projects that captures methane and prevents it from being released into the atmosphere by converting it into either RNG or electrical power for the electrical grid. Its customers for RNG and renewable identification numbers (RIN) include large, long-term owner-operators of landfills and livestock farms, local utilities, and large refiners in the natural gas and refining sectors. Montauk Renewables, Inc. was founded in 1980 and is headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

value.Title: Rexel USA Expands Reach with Revere Electric Supply Acquisition
value.Overview: Global Round Up - ADRs and Depository Receipts, 697words

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJW-S553-S89F-P3YX-00000-00Rexel USA Expands Reach with Revere Electric Supply Acquisition2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisRexel USA Expands Reach with Revere Electric Supply Acquisition

DAILY ADR AND DEPOSITORY RECEIPT REPORT

UNITED STATES EDITION

13 May 2026 09:32 EDT

Rexel USA has announced its agreement to acquire Revere Electric Supply, a prominent electrical distributor based in Mokena, Illinois. The transaction, finalized on 13th May 2026, marks a significant step in Rexel USA's strategy to enhance its regional presence by collaborating with top-tier, service-oriented companies known for their robust customer relationships and technical expertise.

Established in 1919, Revere Electric Supply has evolved from a small distributor into a trusted provider serving NorthernIllinois, Central Illinois, Southeast Wisconsin, and Northwest Indiana. With ten operational locations and a workforce of 375 employees, Revere is recognized for its specialization in industrial automation and its long-standing relationships with electrical contractors. The company's solid foundation in customer service and technical solutions has positioned it as a key player in the Midwest industrial market.

The Eiseman family has been instrumental in shaping Revere's culture and reputation over its century-long history. Current owners Tom Eiseman Sr. and Erik Eiseman have maintained a focus on investing in people and building long-term relationships. Erik Eiseman emphasized the importance of Revere's people and culture, stating that the shared values with Rexel would enable the company to continue flourishing under the new partnership.

Post-acquisition, Revere will retain its name and local identity, ensuring that its strong customer relationships and market presence remain intact. Rexel USA has committed to preserving Revere's culture and leadership team, with John Cady continuing as President while also taking on expanded responsibilities for Rexel's broader Midwest operations. Rexel's Midwest Region President, Hani Masoudi, will support this transition while overseeing other key areas.

Roger Little, CEO of Rexel USA, highlighted the acquisition as a strategic move to bolster their presence in a critical geographic area. He expressed confidence in Revere's capabilities and commitment to customers, emphasizing the potential for growth and innovation as both companies work together. With a robust leadership team and strong industrial automation expertise, Revere is poised for continued success as part of Rexel USA.

SECTION 1 REXEL ACTIVITIES

SECTION 1 Rexel Activities

Activities

Rexel is a leading global distributor of electrical, automation, and digital solutions, headquartered in France, with operations spanning over 30 countries. The company's core business activities center on supplying a wide range of products, including electrical wires, cables, lighting, HVAC systems, and automation technologies, primarily to professionals in the construction, industrial, and services sectors. It also provides value-added services such as technical consulting, project management, and digital tools to enhance efficiency for its customers. Among its fastest-growing segments are renewable energy solutions and digital platforms, fueled by rising demand for sustainable technologies and smart building systems. This growth is supported by Rexel's strategic investments in e-commerce and energy management, positioning the company to capitalize on global trends toward electrification and decarbonization. It is the International Depository Receipt (IDR) market's 10th largest Electronics company by market capitalisation.

value.Title: May 04, 2026: SC Johnson Rolls Out Outdoor Innovations with OFF! and Raid; Space Bag and Babyganics Moves Underway

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JJV-4453-RRRR-73MF-00000-00May 04, 2026: SC Johnson Rolls Out Outdoor Innovations with OFF! and Raid; Space Bag and Babyganics Moves Underway2026-05-13T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisANNOUNCEMENTSMay 04, 2026: SC Johnson Rolls Out Outdoor Innovations with OFF! and Raid; Space Bag and Babyganics Moves Underway

SC Johnson & Son is expanding its outdoor portfolio with new OFF! and Raid products to support spring and summer activities. The announcement, dated 7th May 2026, highlights a line designed to keep patios and gardens bug-free, with prices starting at $5.

In parallel, the company disclosed an agreement to acquire the global Space Bag brand from ITW, expected to finalize in coming months pending U.S. regulatory approvals, with financial terms not disclosed.

SC Johnson has also acquired Babyganics, founded in 2004, a family-focused brand offering skin care, bath care, insect repellent, and cleaning products, aligning with its strategy to strengthen presence in family and personal care. These moves position the company to meet evolving consumer needs.

SECTION 2 SC JOHNSON & SON TOP MANAGEMENT

SECTION 1 SC JOHNSON & SON PROFILE

1.1 ACTIVITIES

SC Johnson & Son, founded in 1886 in Racine, Wisconsin, is a family-owned consumer goods company. With $11 billion in revenue and 13,000 employees, it manufactures household brands like Windex, Glade, and Ziploc, operating in 70 countries. SC Johnson emphasizes sustainability, using renewable energy and reducing plastic waste. Its innovation center develops eco-friendly products, and its transparency in ingredient disclosure sets industry standards. Led by H. Fisk Johnson, the companys philanthropy includes land conservation and education grants. SC Johnsons commitment to quality and environmental responsibility reinforces its global leadership in household goods.

SC Johnson & Son is expanding its outdoor offerings with fresh OFF! and Raid products, timed for the spring and summer season.

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Alliant Energy Corporation

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHM-8873-SHNH-22J5-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Alliant Energy Corporation2026-05-07T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 5-Year Earnings Growth Lags Alliant Energy Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been -0.1% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 36.7% or 9.5% annualized.

In comparison, Alliant Energy Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 5% annualized over 5 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 48.6% or 11.4% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $143.50 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 22.2. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.66 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Alliant Energy Corporation's last trading price was $73.07 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 23.2. With an EPS growth rate of 4.9 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 4.75 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Alliant Energy Corporation is a regulated utility holding company. It provides electric and natural gas services to residential, commercial, and industrial customers in the Midwest. The company operates through its subsidiaries, Interstate Power and Light Company and Wisconsin Power and Light Company. Its primary revenue stream is electric utility operations, which constituted about 93% of third-quarter 2025 revenues. This segment is also its fastest-growing, with revenues up 13% year-over-year, driven by rate increases, higher sales volumes, and expanding data center demand. Gas utility revenues represent approximately 4% of the total, with other utility and non-utility segments comprising the rest. The gas segment also showed growth, increasing 4% amid seasonal fluctuations.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JMJ-WYF3-S1XV-71PH-00000-00letters for 5/6/262026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisOPg. O47letters for 5/6/26

Voucher schools hurting public education

Dear Editor: Wisconsin's constitution does not treat public education as optional. Our elected officials should stop acting as if educating all students is a choice. It is not.

Republicans have asked the court to dismiss a lawsuit from parents and educators. Wisconsin PTA and other plaintiffs argue the state has failed to fund public schools adequately. The suit goes to the heart of a basic question:

Does Wisconsin intend to meet its constitutional duty?

Educational monetary resources are being increasingly shifted away from our public schools toward private/voucher schools. This is not just a local school board issue. It affects communities across the state.

All Wisconsin public school districts are trying to do more with less. These voucher schools are taking money away from public school funding.

Public schools must provide service to every child who walks through the door. Voucher schools have the option of refusal. They do not have to accept all students.

Our public schools provide academics, meals, special education, transportation and stability. Per DPI guidelines, private voucher schools are "only required to offer services to assist students with special needs that it can provide with minor adjustments."

If state leaders believe Wisconsin's children matter, legislators should fully support the public school system.

Children are not a side project. They are our public obligation.

- Patricia Prostco, Kenosha

Youths denied redress on climate change

Dear Editor: Last Thursday, April 23, Dane County Judge Julie Genovese dismissed a lawsuit filed by 15 Wisconsin youths against the Wisconsin state Legislature and the Public Service Commission.

The case challenged the court to find that state laws capping renewable energy requirements and barring utility regulators from considering pollution from new fossil fuel plants are unconstitutional. The plaintiffs contend that increased fossil fuel use causes changes in the Wisconsin climate that result in health concerns like increased incidence of asthma and Lyme disease.

Also, the warming climate in Wisconsin has affected water quality in lakes and streams and reduced annual snowfall, thus depriving the youth access to Wisconsin outdoor activities.

There is a consensus among climate scientists that a warming climate is the result of CO2 emissions to the atmosphere, mainly from the burning of fossil fuels. The Wisconsin youth in the lawsuit all attend school and have been made aware of this knowledge and the consequenses of inaction.

I think the last thing that the elected legislators, appointed regulators or the courts want to reveal to the young people of today is why they continue to ignore or delay action on the existential threat of climate change.

- Harry Bennett, Madison

For the sake of wildlife, fish lead-free

Dear Editor: Before setting out for your favorite fishing spot make sure to check your tackle box.

Every year when lines break and tackle is lost, lead jigs and weights are left behind. That lead builds up in the environment, and wildlife pays the price.

Loons and swans ingest tiny lead pieces while feeding on contaminated sediment or ingesting lead fishing tackle, and eagles will often eat fish that have swallowed lead tackle leading to lead poisoning.

The lead then affects everything from slowing down reaction time (potentially leading to vehicle and wind turbine strikes), attacking eyesight, seizures and shutting down the digestive system - leading to a slow, painful death.

According to the American Bird Conservancy more than 16 million birds are poisoned by lead every year, and a recent study found that nearly half of bald and golden eagles in the United States had chronic levels of lead. The study further showed that these levels are high enough to suppress population growth in both species.

We can take a simple step to preserve the resources we use by fishing lead-free.

Lead-free fishing tackle include materials like tungsten, brass, steel and bismuth, which all have their own unique benefits over lead.

Let's do our part to keep lead off the landscape and out of the water and have a fin-tastic fishing season.

- Dana Herman, Shell Lake

Think before buying backyard chickens

Dear Editor: Getting eggs to hatch into chickens? Here is what your Google search won't tell you.

If you are thinking of buying eggs to hatch into chickens, please know that there is no way to know if you will get an egg laying hen or a rooster.

Many city ordinances do not allow roosters. Do you know if your city allows roosters? What will you do if one of your eggs ends up hatching into a rooster? Did you know these are called "oops roosters?"

Sadly many of these roosters are dumped, abandoned and left to die. Perhaps a lucky one will end up at a farm sanctuary, but most farm sanctuaries and shelters are at capacity.

Please think carefully before buying backyard chickens. Even if you do have all hens, they take time and money to care for, and they should have vet care, similar to a cat or dog. Do you have a plan for the winter? How will you ensure their coop is predator proof? What will you do if raccoons start showing up?

Getting backyard chickens should not be an impulse buy. If you still plan to hatch eggs, opensanctuary.org is a reputable source of information.

- Krysta Koralesky, Waunakee

Yes, Tommy is too old

Dear Editor: John Nichols' piece (" Is Tommy Thompson too old? Never!," April 3) asks if he's too old to run for Wisconsin governor?

Yes.

Come on Cap Times. I, along with many, am tired of old white guys either still hanging on to their jobs - or in this case reapplying?

He was governor in the 80s! Republicans are obsessed with the past and trying to hang onto a time that only benefitted white society. I'm white, by the way.

We're going backwards and not in a good way. Enough already. Focus on current candidates, and Tommy Thompson can just stay retired.

- Margaret Melville, Cedarburg

value.Title: DTE Energy Company 's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Alliant Energy Corporation

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHC-MNG3-RTGT-G0P8-00000-00DTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Alliant Energy Corporation2026-05-06T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisDTE Energy Company's 3-Year Earnings Growth Outperforms Alliant Energy Corporation

NYSE STOCK REPORT

DTE Energy Company's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 8.4% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 41.5% or 14.9% annualized.

In comparison, Alliant Energy Corporation's growth in EPS (earnings per share) has been 4.9% annualized over 3 years. Over the same time period its stock price has gone up 47.5% or 16.5% annualized.

DTE Energy Company's last trading price was $146.73 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 20.8. With an EPS growth rate of 8.4 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 2.49 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Alliant Energy Corporation's last trading price was $73.68 which implies a Price to Earnings multiple of 23.4. With an EPS growth rate of 4.9 that implies a PEG ratio (price/earnings to growth) of 4.79 when using the EPS growth rate of the past 3 years (investors prefer using a 3-year expected growth rate instead but each investor will have a different perspective on future growth).

Company Profile

DTE Energy operates as a Detroit-based diversified energy enterprise involved in the development and management of energy-related businesses and services nationwide. The core business provides vital energy infrastructure, with its largest revenue sources driven by regulated utility operations. These utility operations include an electric company serving 2.3 million customers in Southeast Michigan and a natural gas company serving 1.4 million customers across the state. The fastest-growing segments feature major data center expansions and custom energy solutions, which drive incremental infrastructure investments. Furthermore, the company maintains non-utility energy businesses focused on renewable energy generation and energy marketing and trading. By accelerating the transition to cleaner energy generation, the firm continually delivers reliable and sustainable power to residential, commercial, and large industrial end markets across the region.

Company Profile

Alliant Energy Corporation is a regulated utility holding company. It provides electric and natural gas services to residential, commercial, and industrial customers in the Midwest. The company operates through its subsidiaries, Interstate Power and Light Company and Wisconsin Power and Light Company. Its primary revenue stream is electric utility operations, which constituted about 93% of third-quarter 2025 revenues. This segment is also its fastest-growing, with revenues up 13% year-over-year, driven by rate increases, higher sales volumes, and expanding data center demand. Gas utility revenues represent approximately 4% of the total, with other utility and non-utility segments comprising the rest. The gas segment also showed growth, increasing 4% amid seasonal fluctuations.

value.Title: The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-19T3-RVM1-00JC-00000-00The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisWEB EXTRASThe Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?Juanpablo RamirezFranco for Grist

The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

There are no wind turbines on the Great Lakes and it isn't for lack of wind.

This titanic network of interconnected freshwater lakes with a surface area larger than New England, New York, and New Jersey combined is theoretically ideal for wind farms. Winds sweeping across the lakes are stronger, more consistent, and less turbulent than those over land. The National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimated that the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times their combined annual electricity consumption.

Plus, unlike proposed wind farms along the East Coast, where the federal government controls the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over their lakebeds, Grist reports. While projects would still face federal oversight, some proponents see wind development in the Great Lakes as a potential workaround to President Trump's attacks on the industry.

"If it's done correctly and we're able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand," said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

Still, the vast inland lakes remain untapped largely due to a lack of streamlined permitting processes at the state level and economic hurdles. Offshore wind is expensive, and unlike the East Coast, where a mature wind industry has built specialized ships and ports to build turbines in the sea, the Great Lakes states lack the infrastructure. Ultimately, renewable energy experts say the region doesn't have the legal framework or technical capacity to support such massive projects.

So far, offshore wind power which involves installing turbines in large bodies of water, typically the ocean in the United States, has been concentrated along the Eastern seaboard. Projects are still very rare: Just three are currently operating, with several more under construction, all along the East Coast.

More than a decade ago, President Barack Obama's push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in offshore wind projects particularly in the Great Lakes. The New York Power Authority and cities such as Toronto explored boosting their energy supply with wind power from Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. However, the initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio's Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. After clashing with Ohio regulators over whether turbines could run through the night and battling a court case filed by local residents over impacts to bats and migratory birds, the developers eventually ran out of money and called the project off in 2023.

Building renewable energy offshore in federal waters has now become harder than ever. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind permitting, effectively suspending five large-scale East Coast projects. Although courts later overturned those cancellations, the uncertainty around offshore wind development under the Trump administration has persisted. And since any project on the Great Lakes would still need federal permits and reviews under the Clean Air Act and the Endangered Species Act, among others, wind developers have largely backed off their plans.

"That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois," said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, an offshore wind developer focused on the Great Lakes. "Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry."

Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it's a bad time for business. His company, which in the past has submitted comments to the Illinois power regulators related to the deployment of offshore wind on Lake Michigan, has temporarily paused pursuing work on the Great Lakes.

"Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from WashingtonD.C.," Lanard said. "It's not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don't know how they're going to balance their budgets."

Though Illinois' jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality. Given these challenges, he estimated it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.

"I'm looking at five to seven years from 2029," Lanard said.

For years, state Representative Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois' shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

This story is a partnership between Grist and WBEZ, a public radio station serving the Chicago metropolitan region.

This story was produced by Grist and reviewed and distributed by Stacker.

urn:contentItem:6JHB-19T3-RVDS-P4F5-00000-00The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisWEB EXTRASThe Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?Juanpablo RamirezFranco for Grist

The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

There are no wind turbines on the Great Lakes and it isn't for lack of wind.

This titanic network of interconnected freshwater lakes with a surface area larger than New England, New York, and New Jersey combined is theoretically ideal for wind farms. Winds sweeping across the lakes are stronger, more consistent, and less turbulent than those over land. The National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimated that the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times their combined annual electricity consumption.

Plus, unlike proposed wind farms along the East Coast, where the federal government controls the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over their lakebeds, Grist reports. While projects would still face federal oversight, some proponents see wind development in the Great Lakes as a potential workaround to President Trump's attacks on the industry.

"If it's done correctly and we're able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand," said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

Still, the vast inland lakes remain untapped largely due to a lack of streamlined permitting processes at the state level and economic hurdles. Offshore wind is expensive, and unlike the East Coast, where a mature wind industry has built specialized ships and ports to build turbines in the sea, the Great Lakes states lack the infrastructure. Ultimately, renewable energy experts say the region doesn't have the legal framework or technical capacity to support such massive projects.

So far, offshore wind power which involves installing turbines in large bodies of water, typically the ocean in the United States, has been concentrated along the Eastern seaboard. Projects are still very rare: Just three are currently operating, with several more under construction, all along the East Coast.

More than a decade ago, President Barack Obama's push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in offshore wind projects particularly in the Great Lakes. The New York Power Authority and cities such as Toronto explored boosting their energy supply with wind power from Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. However, the initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio's Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. After clashing with Ohio regulators over whether turbines could run through the night and battling a court case filed by local residents over impacts to bats and migratory birds, the developers eventually ran out of money and called the project off in 2023.

Building renewable energy offshore in federal waters has now become harder than ever. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind permitting, effectively suspending five large-scale East Coast projects. Although courts later overturned those cancellations, the uncertainty around offshore wind development under the Trump administration has persisted. And since any project on the Great Lakes would still need federal permits and reviews under the Clean Air Act and the Endangered Species Act, among others, wind developers have largely backed off their plans.

"That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois," said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, an offshore wind developer focused on the Great Lakes. "Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry."

Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it's a bad time for business. His company, which in the past has submitted comments to the Illinois power regulators related to the deployment of offshore wind on Lake Michigan, has temporarily paused pursuing work on the Great Lakes.

"Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from WashingtonD.C.," Lanard said. "It's not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don't know how they're going to balance their budgets."

Though Illinois' jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality. Given these challenges, he estimated it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.

"I'm looking at five to seven years from 2029," Lanard said.

For years, state Representative Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois' shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

This story is a partnership between Grist and WBEZ, a public radio station serving the Chicago metropolitan region.

This story was produced by Grist and reviewed and distributed by Stacker.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

urn:contentItem:6JHB-19T3-RVM1-00G7-00000-00The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisWEB EXTRASThe Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?Juanpablo RamirezFranco for Grist

The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

There are no wind turbines on the Great Lakes and it isn't for lack of wind.

This titanic network of interconnected freshwater lakes with a surface area larger than New England, New York, and New Jersey combined is theoretically ideal for wind farms. Winds sweeping across the lakes are stronger, more consistent, and less turbulent than those over land. The National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimated that the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times their combined annual electricity consumption.

Plus, unlike proposed wind farms along the East Coast, where the federal government controls the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over their lakebeds, Grist reports. While projects would still face federal oversight, some proponents see wind development in the Great Lakes as a potential workaround to President Trump's attacks on the industry.

"If it's done correctly and we're able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand," said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

Still, the vast inland lakes remain untapped largely due to a lack of streamlined permitting processes at the state level and economic hurdles. Offshore wind is expensive, and unlike the East Coast, where a mature wind industry has built specialized ships and ports to build turbines in the sea, the Great Lakes states lack the infrastructure. Ultimately, renewable energy experts say the region doesn't have the legal framework or technical capacity to support such massive projects.

So far, offshore wind power which involves installing turbines in large bodies of water, typically the ocean in the United States, has been concentrated along the Eastern seaboard. Projects are still very rare: Just three are currently operating, with several more under construction, all along the East Coast.

More than a decade ago, President Barack Obama's push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in offshore wind projects particularly in the Great Lakes. The New York Power Authority and cities such as Toronto explored boosting their energy supply with wind power from Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. However, the initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio's Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. After clashing with Ohio regulators over whether turbines could run through the night and battling a court case filed by local residents over impacts to bats and migratory birds, the developers eventually ran out of money and called the project off in 2023.

Building renewable energy offshore in federal waters has now become harder than ever. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind permitting, effectively suspending five large-scale East Coast projects. Although courts later overturned those cancellations, the uncertainty around offshore wind development under the Trump administration has persisted. And since any project on the Great Lakes would still need federal permits and reviews under the Clean Air Act and the Endangered Species Act, among others, wind developers have largely backed off their plans.

"That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois," said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, an offshore wind developer focused on the Great Lakes. "Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry."

Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it's a bad time for business. His company, which in the past has submitted comments to the Illinois power regulators related to the deployment of offshore wind on Lake Michigan, has temporarily paused pursuing work on the Great Lakes.

"Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from WashingtonD.C.," Lanard said. "It's not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don't know how they're going to balance their budgets."

Though Illinois' jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality. Given these challenges, he estimated it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.

"I'm looking at five to seven years from 2029," Lanard said.

For years, state Representative Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois' shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

This story is a partnership between Grist and WBEZ, a public radio station serving the Chicago metropolitan region.

This story was produced by Grist and reviewed and distributed by Stacker.

The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

value.Title: The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-19T3-RVM1-0285-00000-00The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisWEB EXTRASThe Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?Juanpablo RamirezFranco for Grist

The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

There are no wind turbines on the Great Lakes and it isn't for lack of wind.

This titanic network of interconnected freshwater lakes with a surface area larger than New England, New York, and New Jersey combined is theoretically ideal for wind farms. Winds sweeping across the lakes are stronger, more consistent, and less turbulent than those over land. The National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimated that the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times their combined annual electricity consumption.

Plus, unlike proposed wind farms along the East Coast, where the federal government controls the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over their lakebeds, Grist reports. While projects would still face federal oversight, some proponents see wind development in the Great Lakes as a potential workaround to President Trump's attacks on the industry.

"If it's done correctly and we're able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand," said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

Still, the vast inland lakes remain untapped largely due to a lack of streamlined permitting processes at the state level and economic hurdles. Offshore wind is expensive, and unlike the East Coast, where a mature wind industry has built specialized ships and ports to build turbines in the sea, the Great Lakes states lack the infrastructure. Ultimately, renewable energy experts say the region doesn't have the legal framework or technical capacity to support such massive projects.

So far, offshore wind power which involves installing turbines in large bodies of water, typically the ocean in the United States, has been concentrated along the Eastern seaboard. Projects are still very rare: Just three are currently operating, with several more under construction, all along the East Coast.

More than a decade ago, President Barack Obama's push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in offshore wind projects particularly in the Great Lakes. The New York Power Authority and cities such as Toronto explored boosting their energy supply with wind power from Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. However, the initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio's Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. After clashing with Ohio regulators over whether turbines could run through the night and battling a court case filed by local residents over impacts to bats and migratory birds, the developers eventually ran out of money and called the project off in 2023.

Building renewable energy offshore in federal waters has now become harder than ever. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind permitting, effectively suspending five large-scale East Coast projects. Although courts later overturned those cancellations, the uncertainty around offshore wind development under the Trump administration has persisted. And since any project on the Great Lakes would still need federal permits and reviews under the Clean Air Act and the Endangered Species Act, among others, wind developers have largely backed off their plans.

"That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois," said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, an offshore wind developer focused on the Great Lakes. "Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry."

Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it's a bad time for business. His company, which in the past has submitted comments to the Illinois power regulators related to the deployment of offshore wind on Lake Michigan, has temporarily paused pursuing work on the Great Lakes.

"Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from WashingtonD.C.," Lanard said. "It's not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don't know how they're going to balance their budgets."

Though Illinois' jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality. Given these challenges, he estimated it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.

"I'm looking at five to seven years from 2029," Lanard said.

For years, state Representative Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois' shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

value.Title: MGE Energy Reports First-Quarter 2026 Earnings

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JH6-B5D3-RWR5-R0KY-00000-00MGE Energy Reports First-Quarter 2026 Earnings2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:09ZLexisNexisMGE Energy Reports First-Quarter 2026 EarningsMADISON, Wis.

MGE Energy, Inc. (Nasdaq: MGEE), today reported financial results for the first quarter of 2026.

MGE Energy's GAAP (Generally Accepted Accounting Principles) earnings for the first quarter of 2026 were $48.5 million, or $1.32 per share, compared to $41.6 million, or $1.14 per share, for the same period in the prior year.

Electric segment earnings increased $5.5 million for 2026 compared to 2025, a result of strategic capital investments that grew rate base. This growth remains largely driven by the successful deployment of key renewable energy projects.

Gas net income exhibited steady performance, with minimal variation compared to the first quarter of 2025.

MGE Energy, Inc.(In thousands, except per-share amounts)(Unaudited)Three Months Ended March 31,20262025Operating revenues$242,703$218,970Operating income$53,152$52,865Net income$48,481$41,592Earnings per share - basic$1.32$1.14Earnings per share - diluted$1.32$1.14Weighted average shares outstanding - basic36,59036,511Weighted average shares outstanding - diluted36,62036,539

About MGE Energy

MGE Energy is a public utility holding company. Its principal subsidiary, Madison Gas and Electric, generates and distributes electricity to 170,000 customers in Dane County, Wis., and purchases and distributes natural gas to 180,000 customers in seven south-central and western Wisconsin counties. MGE's roots in the Madison area date back more than 150 years.

Forward-looking Statements

This press release contains forward-looking statements within the meaning of the Private Securities Litigation Reform Act of 1995, as amended. Such forward-looking statements are based on MGE Energy's current expectations, estimates and assumptions regarding future events, which are inherently uncertain. We caution you not to place undue reliance on any forward-looking statements, which are made as of the date of this press release. We undertake no obligation to revise or update publicly any such forward-looking statements to reflect any change in expectations or in events, conditions or circumstances on which any such statements may be based. For a further description of the risks and uncertainties that could cause actual results to differ from those expressed in these forward-looking statements, as well as risks relating to our business in general, please refer to the "Risk Factors" sections in our Annual Report on Form 10-K for the year ended December 31, 2025, filed with the Securities and Exchange Commission.

urn:contentItem:6JGS-BGJ3-RV13-X1XJ-00000-00Analyzing Environmental Power OTCMKTS:EPGRQ and Montauk Renewables NASDAQ:MNTK2026-05-03T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:04:18ZLexisNexisAnalyzing Environmental Power OTCMKTS:EPGRQ and Montauk Renewables NASDAQ:MNTK

Environmental Power OTCMKTS:EPGRQ - Get Free Report and Montauk Renewables NASDAQ:MNTK - Get Free Report are both energy companies, but which is the better investment? We will contrast the two businesses based on the strength of their valuation, profitability, risk, analyst recommendations, institutional ownership, dividends and earnings.

Analyst Recommendations

This is a breakdown of recent ratings and recommmendations for Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables, as provided by MarketBeat. Sell RatingsHold RatingsBuy RatingsStrong Buy RatingsRating ScoreEnvironmental Power00000.00Montauk Renewables13012.20

Montauk Renewables has a consensus target price of $1.80, indicating a potential upside of 28.57%. Given Montauk Renewables' stronger consensus rating and higher probable upside, analysts plainly believe Montauk Renewables is more favorable than Environmental Power.

Earnings and Valuation

This table compares Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables"s gross revenue, earnings per share and valuation. Gross RevenuePrice/Sales RatioNet IncomeEarnings Per SharePrice/Earnings RatioEnvironmental PowerN/AN/AN/AN/AN/AMontauk Renewables$176.38 million1.14$1.75 million$0.0270.00

Montauk Renewables has higher revenue and earnings than Environmental Power.

Profitability

This table compares Environmental Power and Montauk Renewables' net margins, return on equity and return on assets. Net MarginsReturn on EquityReturn on AssetsEnvironmental PowerN/AN/AN/AMontauk Renewables0.15%0.10%0.07%

Institutional and Insider Ownership

16.4% of Montauk Renewables shares are owned by institutional investors. 18.8% of Environmental Power shares are owned by insiders. Comparatively, 54.3% of Montauk Renewables shares are owned by insiders. Strong institutional ownership is an indication that endowments, large money managers and hedge funds believe a company is poised for long-term growth.

Summary

Montauk Renewables beats Environmental Power on 9 of the 9 factors compared between the two stocks.

About Environmental Power Get Free Report

Environmental Power Corporation engages in the development, ownership, and operation of renewable energy production facilities in the United States. The company develops renewable energy facilities for the production and commercial application of methane-rich biogas produced from animal, food industry, and other organic wastes. The biogas could be sold to end-users or used to produce pipeline-grade methane, which is referred as renewable natural gas, liquefied natural gas, compressed natural gas, and renewable electrical energy or thermal energy, as well as to produce other useful by-products. Environmental Power Corporation operates three single digester facilities in Wisconsin. The company was founded in 1982 and is headquartered in Tarrytown, New York.

About Montauk Renewables Get Free Report

Montauk Renewables, Inc., a renewable energy company, engages in recovery and processing of biogas from landfills and other non-fossil fuel sources. It operates in two segments, Renewable Natural Gas and Renewable Electricity Generation. The company develops, owns, and operates renewable natural gas RNG projects that captures methane and prevents it from being released into the atmosphere by converting it into either RNG or electrical power for the electrical grid. Its customers for RNG and renewable identification numbers RIN include large, long-term owner-operators of landfills and livestock farms, local utilities, and large refiners in the natural gas and refining sectors. Montauk Renewables, Inc. was founded in 1980 and is headquartered in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.

Document.Content:
urn:contentItem:6JHB-B2C3-S487-92MG-00000-00The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?2026-05-05T00:00:00Z2026-05-28T16:18:08ZLexisNexisBEYOND ST. LAWRENCE COUNTYThe Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimatedJUANPABLO RAMIREZ-FRANCO FOR GRIST

The Great Lakes are ideal for wind energy. So where is it?

There are no wind turbines on the Great Lakes and it isn't for lack of wind.

This titanic network of interconnected freshwater lakes with a surface area larger than New England, New York, and New Jersey combined is theoretically ideal for wind farms. Winds sweeping across the lakes are stronger, more consistent, and less turbulent than those over land. The National Laboratory of the Rockies has estimated that the Great Lakes states have enough offshore wind potential to generate more than three times their combined annual electricity consumption.

Plus, unlike proposed wind farms along the East Coast, where the federal government controls the seabed, Great Lakes states have jurisdiction over their lakebeds, Grist reports. While projects would still face federal oversight, some proponents see wind development in the Great Lakes as a potential workaround to President Trump's attacks on the industry.

"If it's done correctly and we're able to harness even a fraction of that, we could offset a lot of electricity demand," said Melissa Scanlan, director of the Center for Water Policy at the University of Wisconsin-Milwaukee.

Still, the vast inland lakes remain untapped largely due to a lack of streamlined permitting processes at the state level and economic hurdles. Offshore wind is expensive, and unlike the East Coast, where a mature wind industry has built specialized ships and ports to build turbines in the sea, the Great Lakes states lack the infrastructure. Ultimately, renewable energy experts say the region doesn't have the legal framework or technical capacity to support such massive projects.

So far, offshore wind power which involves installing turbines in large bodies of water, typically the ocean in the United States, has been concentrated along the Eastern seaboard. Projects are still very rare: Just three are currently operating, with several more under construction, all along the East Coast.

More than a decade ago, President Barack Obama's push for renewable energy buoyed a short-lived boom in offshore wind projects particularly in the Great Lakes. The New York Power Authority and cities such as Toronto explored boosting their energy supply with wind power from Lake Ontario and Lake Erie. However, the initial boost only propelled one project forward: Ohio's Icebreaker Wind in Lake Erie. After clashing with Ohio regulators over whether turbines could run through the night and battling a court case filed by local residents over impacts to bats and migratory birds, the developers eventually ran out of money and called the project off in 2023.

Building renewable energy offshore in federal waters has now become harder than ever. On his first day back in office, President Donald Trump signed a memorandum shutting down all offshore wind permitting, effectively suspending five large-scale East Coast projects. Although courts later overturned those cancellations, the uncertainty around offshore wind development under the Trump administration has persisted. And since any project on the Great Lakes would still need federal permits and reviews under the Clean Air Act and the Endangered Species Act, among others, wind developers have largely backed off their plans.

"That told us that this was not the time to reintroduce floating offshore wind in Illinois," said Jim Lanard, co-founder of Magellan Wind, an offshore wind developer focused on the Great Lakes. "Rather, we needed to face the existential threat to the industry."

Lanard said the regulatory uncertainty means it's a bad time for business. His company, which in the past has submitted comments to the Illinois power regulators related to the deployment of offshore wind on Lake Michigan, has temporarily paused pursuing work on the Great Lakes.

"Governors and legislatures are so focused on trying to figure out how to run their governments with the reduced support coming from WashingtonD.C.," Lanard said. "It's not time for them to add additional burdens onto their staff, who don't know how they're going to balance their budgets."

Though Illinois' jurisdiction over part of Lake Michigan presents some opportunities to work around Trump, Lanard said developers are not going to come to Illinois until there is a federal policy to protect the industry from whiplash elections. Moreover, Illinois and the other Great Lakes states have yet to establish a substantial legal framework to make local offshore wind farms a reality. Given these challenges, he estimated it will be some time before there are any wind farms on the Great Lakes.

"I'm looking at five to seven years from 2029," Lanard said.

For years, state Representative Marcus Evans Jr. has championed a plan to make Illinois' shores home to the first offshore wind farm in the Great Lakes. The legislation, known as the Illinois Rust Belt to Green Belt Pilot Program Act, was designed to direct state planners to solicit proposals for a new utility-scale offshore wind farm and begin purchasing offshore wind power. First introduced by Evans in 2022, the measure has long failed to pick up enough momentum to break out of the statehouse. In 2025, Illinois Senator Robert Peters refiled the bill, but it didn't receive a committee assignment. Evans said he decided against refiling the bill this year.

"I didn't have the support," said Evans, a South Side Chicago Democrat. "I used my energy this year to focus on some other items, but it's still something that I think is important," he added.

Since the bill would require a localized industry to build, transport, and upkeep the turbines, Evans saw the push as a huge boon for the region's clean energy workforce one that the state of Illinois has long been trying to prop up.

Illinois passed the Climate and Equitable Jobs Act in 2021, which committed the state to investing $80 million annually to build out workforce training hubs focused on clean energy-related jobs. The same bill also put the state on track to transition its energy sector to 100 percent clean energy by 2050. Evans had hoped his initiative could help achieve both goals at once.

He is still optimistic that Lake Michigan will be next.

"I will file [a bill] in the future," he said.

This story is a partnership between Grist and WBEZ, a public radio station serving the Chicago metropolitan region.

This story was produced by Grist and reviewed and distributed by Stacker.
